title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Neurology, Emergency Medicine, Critical Care
objectives: Apply diagnostic criteria and CSF findings
Identify variants including Miller Fisher syndrome

vignette.text:
A 38-year-old man presents with progressive bilateral leg weakness over 5 days. He had a respiratory infection 2 weeks ago. He now has difficulty climbing stairs and notes tingling in his feet.

Past Medical History: None

Medications: None

physicalExam.neuro: Symmetric proximal and distal weakness (3/5 bilateral LE, 4/5 bilateral UE), absent ankle reflexes, decreased knee reflexes, intact sensation, no facial weakness

questions.stem: This patient has progressive ascending weakness after infection.
questions.question: What are the clinical features of Guillain-Barré Syndrome?

options.text: Sensory loss is always prominent

GBS CLINICAL FEATURES: CLASSIC PRESENTATION: Progressive, symmetric weakness; Ascending pattern (legs → arms → face/respiratory); Hyporeflexia or areflexia (hallmark); Reaches nadir by 4 weeks (usually 2-3 weeks); ANTECEDENT ILLNESS (2/3 of cases): Respiratory or GI infection 1-4 weeks prior; Campylobacter jejuni (most common), CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma; COVID-19, influenza vaccination (rare); SENSORY: Paresthesias, pain common; Sensory loss mild or absent on exam; AUTONOMIC (70%): Tachycardia, bradycardia; BP fluctuations; Urinary retention; Ileus; VARIANTS: Miller Fisher (ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, areflexia); AMAN (pure motor, axonal); AIDP (classic demyelinating)
Weakness is typically asymmetric
Hyperreflexia is characteristic

questions.explanation: GBS Clinical Features: Progressive symmetric ascending weakness with hyporeflexia/areflexia. Usually reaches nadir by 2-4 weeks. Antecedent infection in 2/3 (Campylobacter most common). Sensory symptoms (paresthesias, pain) common but sensory loss mild. Autonomic dysfunction in 70%. Variants include Miller Fisher (ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, areflexia).

sources.text: StatPearls: Guillain-Barré Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What are the diagnostic findings in GBS?

options.text: GBS DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS: CSF - ALBUMINOCYTOLOGIC DISSOCIATION: Elevated protein (>45 mg/dL); Normal cell count (<10 cells/µL); May be normal in first week (protein rises over days); NERVE CONDUCTION STUDIES/EMG: Demyelinating pattern (AIDP): Prolonged distal latencies; Conduction block; Slow conduction velocities; Axonal pattern (AMAN/AMSAN): Reduced amplitudes; CLINICAL CRITERIA: Progressive weakness in >1 limb; Areflexia or hyporeflexia; Progression over days to 4 weeks; Symmetric involvement; Mild sensory symptoms; Cranial nerve involvement; Autonomic dysfunction; EXCLUDES GBS: Distinct sensory level; Marked bowel/bladder dysfunction early; CSF pleocytosis (>50 cells)
CSF shows elevated WBC count
Protein is always elevated immediately
EMG is not helpful

questions.explanation: GBS Diagnosis: CSF shows ALBUMINOCYTOLOGIC DISSOCIATION (elevated protein, normal cells) - may be normal first week. NCS/EMG shows demyelinating (AIDP) or axonal (AMAN) pattern. Clinical criteria: progressive symmetric weakness, areflexia, reaches nadir by 4 weeks. Red flags against GBS: sensory level, early severe bladder dysfunction, CSF pleocytosis >50.

sources.text: StatPearls: Guillain-Barré Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: When should GBS patients be intubated?

options.text: RESPIRATORY MONITORING IN GBS: 20-30% require mechanical ventilation; MONITOR: FVC (forced vital capacity) q4-6h; NIF (negative inspiratory force); Single breath count; INTUBATION CRITERIA (20/30/40 rule): FVC <20 mL/kg (or <1L); NIF weaker than -30 cmH2O; >30% decline from baseline; FVC <15 mL/kg = imminent respiratory failure; PREDICTORS OF VENTILATION (Erasmus GBS Respiratory Insufficiency Score): Days from onset to admission; Bulbar weakness; Facial weakness; Proximal arm weakness; OTHER INDICATIONS: Bulbar dysfunction with aspiration risk; Severe autonomic instability; Rapid progression; DO NOT rely on oxygen saturation - late sign!
Respiratory failure is rare (<5%)
Only intubate when O2 sat drops
FVC monitoring is unnecessary

questions.explanation: Respiratory Monitoring: 20-30% of GBS patients need ventilation. Monitor FVC q4-6h. INTUBATION using 20/30/40 rule: FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF weaker than -30, >30% decline. FVC <15 mL/kg indicates imminent failure. Predictors: rapid progression, bulbar/facial weakness. DO NOT rely on O2 sat - late sign of respiratory failure!

sources.text: StatPearls: Guillain-Barré Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the treatment for GBS?

options.text: GBS TREATMENT: IMMUNOTHERAPY (start within 2-4 weeks of onset): IVIG: 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days (2g/kg total); Preferred in many centers (ease of administration); OR Plasmapheresis (PLEX): 5 exchanges over 1-2 weeks; Equally effective to IVIG; May be preferred in severe cases; DO NOT combine IVIG + PLEX (no added benefit); DO NOT use corticosteroids (ineffective, possibly harmful); SUPPORTIVE CARE: DVT prophylaxis; Pain management (neuropathic pain common); PT/OT; Autonomic monitoring; Nutrition (if bulbar); MILD CASES (ambulatory): May not require immunotherapy; Close monitoring essential; PROGNOSIS: 80% walk independently at 1 year; 3-5% mortality; 5-10% have significant residual deficits
Wait until respiratory failure to treat
Combine IVIG and plasmapheresis for best results
Corticosteroids are first-line treatment

questions.explanation: GBS Treatment: IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days OR Plasmapheresis 5 exchanges - equally effective, do NOT combine. Start within 2-4 weeks of onset. Corticosteroids are NOT effective. Supportive care: DVT prophylaxis, pain management, respiratory monitoring, PT/OT. Prognosis: 80% walk at 1 year, 3-5% mortality.

sources.text: StatPearls: Guillain-Barré Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: CSF shows protein 85 mg/dL, WBC 3 cells/µL.
questions.question: What is the complete approach?

options.text: Respiratory monitoring unnecessary

Complete approach: (1) DIAGNOSIS: Classic GBS - ascending weakness + areflexia + antecedent infection + albuminocytologic dissociation; (2) ADMIT TO ICU or step-down for monitoring; (3) RESPIRATORY MONITORING: FVC q4-6h initially; Intubate if FVC <20 mL/kg or rapid decline; (4) TREATMENT: IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days (start early); OR Plasmapheresis if IVIG contraindicated; (5) SUPPORTIVE: DVT prophylaxis; Pain management; PT/OT consult; Autonomic monitoring; (6) MONITOR FOR: Respiratory failure, autonomic instability, aspiration; (7) PROGNOSIS DISCUSSION: Most recover well but may take months; Prepare for potential prolonged ICU stay
Start steroids immediately
Outpatient management is appropriate

questions.explanation: Complete Approach: Confirm diagnosis (ascending weakness, areflexia, CSF albuminocytologic dissociation). Admit for monitoring, FVC q4-6h. Start IVIG 0.4g/kg × 5 days or plasmapheresis early. Supportive care: DVT prophylaxis, pain, PT/OT. Monitor for respiratory failure and autonomic instability. Prognosis: 80% walk at 1 year but recovery takes months.

sources.text: StatPearls: Guillain-Barré Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected guillain-barré syndrome?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in guillain-barré syndrome. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of guillain-barré syndrome. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing guillain-barré syndrome, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with optic neuritis. MRI brain shows 4 periventricular white matter lesions. CSF shows oligoclonal bands. Which criteria are met for MS diagnosis?

options.text: Dissemination in space (DIS) is met; need evidence of dissemination in time (DIT) for definitive diagnosis
options.explanation: Per the 2017 McDonald criteria, DIS requires ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 of 4 MS-typical regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). With periventricular lesions + optic neuritis (separate CNS region), DIS is met. DIT can be met by a new T2 or gadolinium-enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI, or simultaneously by both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions.

options.text: Both DIS and DIT are met — diagnose MS now
options.explanation: DIS is met but DIT requires evidence of lesions at different time points. The initial scan alone (even with oligoclonal bands) cannot establish DIT unless both enhancing AND non-enhancing lesions are present simultaneously.

options.text: Neither criterion is met — optic neuritis is a clinically isolated syndrome only
options.explanation: While optic neuritis alone is a CIS, the 4 periventricular white matter lesions establish DIS. CIS + DIS constitutes significant risk for MS.

options.text: CSF oligoclonal bands alone establish the diagnosis regardless of MRI findings
options.explanation: Oligoclonal bands support the diagnosis and can substitute for DIT in certain scenarios per 2017 McDonald criteria, but they do not independently establish the diagnosis without meeting DIS.

sections.title: Clinical Approach
sections.content: Systematic approach to Guillain-Barré Syndrome including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content: Evidence-based clinical pearls for Guillain-Barré Syndrome.
